title: Four Blocks Course — Chapter 02: Depression
subtitle: Formula, Mental Contamination, Disputation Process, and Chapters 1–5 Applied to Depression
source: Dr. Vincent E. Parr — My Four Blocks Course

chunks.title: Chapter 1 — Mental Contamination Applied to Depression
chunks.key_concept: Depression is caused by mental contamination — mistaking thoughts and beliefs for facts.
chunks.application: Depression often stems from negative thought loops about the past, present, or future. These thoughts are not facts but stories told by the mind. Example: I will never be happy again because I failed at my job. This assumes permanence and ignores the possibility of change.
chunks.solution: Train the Observer to notice negative thought loops and recognize them as temporary. Let go of mental contamination by observing and disputing irrational beliefs.

chunks.title: Chapter 2 — Three Insights Applied to Depression

insights.insight: Insight 1 — You Create 100% of Your Depression
insights.application: Depression is not caused by external events but by beliefs about those events. Example: I am depressed because I lost my job. Dispute: I feel depressed because I believe losing my job means I am a failure.
insights.key_takeaway: You are responsible for your depression because you create it through your thoughts and beliefs.

insights.insight: Insight 2 — Become Aware of How You Create Depression
insights.application: Use the ABC Model to trace depression to its source. A = losing your job. B = I will never find another job and I am worthless. C = depression.
insights.key_takeaway: Becoming aware of your beliefs allows you to identify and dispute the irrational thoughts that fuel depression.

insights.insight: Insight 3 — Dispute and Let Go Rapidly
insights.application: Dispute the irrational belief causing depression. Replace: I am worthless because I lost my job. With: I do not like losing my job but I am still a capable person who can find another opportunity.
insights.key_takeaway: Disputing irrational beliefs allows you to release depression and replace it with acceptance and self-compassion.

chunks.title: Chapter 3 — The ABCs Applied to Depression
chunks.key_concept: Emotions are created through the ABC Model. A = Activating Event. B = Belief. C = Consequence.

example.B: I am worthless because I lost my job.

chunks.application: Depression is caused by A-to-C thinking, where the activating event is mistakenly believed to directly cause depression. In reality it is the belief (B) about the event that creates the emotional consequence (C).
chunks.solution: Train the mind to identify and dispute irrational beliefs at B to change the emotional response at C.

chunks.title: Chapter 4 — Seven Irrational Beliefs Applied to Depression
chunks.key_concept: Depression is caused by irrational beliefs. Each of the seven beliefs can fuel depression and must be disputed.

beliefs_applied.example: Losing my job made me depressed!
beliefs_applied.dispute: Losing my job did not make me depressed. My thoughts about losing my job are creating my depression.

beliefs_applied.example: Losing my job is the worst thing that could happen!
beliefs_applied.dispute: Losing my job is unpleasant but not the worst thing. I can handle it.
I do not like being unemployed but I can stand it. I have stood worse before.

beliefs_applied.example: I should have kept my job!
beliefs_applied.dispute: I would have preferred to keep my job but I can accept that I did not.

beliefs_applied.example: I lost my job so I am a failure!
beliefs_applied.dispute: Losing my job does not make me a failure. It is just one event.

beliefs_applied.example: I will never find another job!
beliefs_applied.dispute: I do not know the future. I can try my best to find another job.

beliefs_applied.example: I worked so hard and deserved to keep my job!
beliefs_applied.dispute: Working hard increases chances of success but does not guarantee it.

components.label: Hopelessness
components.definition: Believing the past, present, and future are bleak and unchangeable.
components.example: I have always been unhappy and I will never be happy again.
components.dispute: I have had happy moments before and I can create them again.

components.label: Helplessness
components.definition: Believing you are powerless to change your situation.
components.example: I cannot do anything to fix my life.
components.dispute: I can take small steps to improve my situation.

components.label: Need
components.definition: Believing you need something external to be happy.
components.example: I need my job to feel good about myself.
components.dispute: I would prefer to have my job but I can still find happiness without it.

chunks.title: Depression — Chapters 1–5 Integrated Summary

integrated_steps.action: Recognize that depression is caused by beliefs, not the event itself.
Accept responsibility for depression, understand how beliefs create it, and learn to dispute and let go of those beliefs.
Identify the activating event (A), the belief (B), and the consequence (C). Focus on disputing the belief.
Look for irrational beliefs like shoulds, awfulizing, and ICSI and dispute them with rational thinking.
Identify feelings of hopelessness, helplessness, and need. Dispute them with rational thinking and replace them with acceptance and self-compassion.

chunks.closing_principle: By applying these principles you can reduce depression, let go of negative thought patterns, and cultivate a mind of peace and happiness.

retrieve_by_context.context: depression formula OR hopelessness OR helplessness OR need
